docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Two of the most important thinking frameworks in the modern world are the scientific method and the engineering design process. At first glance, they seem similar — both are systematic, both involve testing ideas, and both drive innovation. Yet they answer fundamentally different questions. Science asks why something happens. Engineering asks how we can make something happen, or how we can make something work better.

This chapter unpacks both frameworks, compares them side by side, and then introduces a third lens — systems thinking — that engineers use to understand the organizations and industries they work within. All three frameworks will appear throughout your practicum project, from the moment you identify a problem to the day you present your final solution.

docs.title: The Scientific Method
docs.text:
The scientific method is a structured process for generating reliable knowledge about the natural world through observation, experimentation, and evidence-based reasoning. It has been refined over centuries and forms the backbone of all natural and social sciences.

The classical steps of the scientific method are:

  1. Observation — Notice a phenomenon or ask a question about the natural world.
  2. Background Research — Gather existing knowledge related to the question.
  3. Hypothesis — Propose a testable, falsifiable explanation for the observed phenomenon.
  4. Experiment Design — Plan a controlled experiment to test the hypothesis.
  5. Data Collection — Conduct the experiment and record measurements systematically.
  6. Analysis — Examine the data to identify patterns and draw conclusions.
  7. Communication — Share findings with the scientific community for peer review and replication.

The method is inherently circular: conclusions from one experiment often generate new questions, launching the cycle again. This iterative nature is what allows science to self-correct over time.

docs.title: Hypothesis Testing
docs.text:
Before we move to engineering, it is worth pausing on one of the most important steps in the scientific method: hypothesis testing. A hypothesis is a specific, testable prediction about a relationship between variables. The key word is testable — a hypothesis must be capable of being proven wrong by evidence, a property philosophers of science call falsifiability.

A well-formed hypothesis follows the "If … then … because …" structure:

  • If the independent variable is changed in a specific way,
  • then the dependent variable will respond in a predictable way,
  • because a known mechanism or principle explains the connection.

Example: "If we double the insulation thickness on a pipe, then heat loss will decrease by at least 40%, because heat conduction is inversely proportional to material thickness."

Scientists compare results against a null hypothesis — the assumption that no relationship exists — and use statistical tests to determine whether the evidence is strong enough to reject it. Engineers sometimes borrow hypothesis-testing language when running controlled experiments during prototyping, but their goal is usually practical performance rather than knowledge generation.

docs.title: Diagram: Hypothesis Testing Decision Tree
docs.text:
Interactive Hypothesis Testing Decision Tree

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: hypothesis-testing-tree Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will explain (Bloom L2) the logical structure of hypothesis testing by navigating an interactive decision tree that traces a hypothesis from formation through accept/reject outcome.

Canvas: 800 × 480 px, responsive to window resize.

Layout: A vertical flowchart with six node levels. Each node is a rounded rectangle with a label and a brief description (visible on hover). Directional arrows connect nodes. Two branch paths diverge at the "Analyze Results" node — one leading to "Reject Null Hypothesis" (green) and one to "Fail to Reject Null Hypothesis" (amber).

Nodes (top to bottom): 1. Observe a Phenomenon 2. Form a Hypothesis (If/Then/Because) 3. Design a Controlled Experiment 4. Collect Data 5. Analyze Results → branch point - Left branch: Evidence is statistically significant → Reject Null Hypothesis → New Knowledge Generated - Right branch: Evidence is not significant → Fail to Reject Null Hypothesis → Revise Hypothesis

Interaction: Clicking any node opens a side panel (200 px wide, right side) showing: (a) the step name, (b) a 2-sentence explanation at Senior High reading level, and (c) a real-world engineering example of that step.

Color scheme: Nodes are SteelBlue; the two outcome nodes use DarkGreen (reject) and DarkGoldenrod (fail to reject). Arrows are dark gray.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/hypothesis-testing-tree/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 520 px height.

docs.title: The Engineering Design Process
docs.text:
The engineering design process is a systematic, iterative approach for solving problems and creating artifacts — products, structures, systems, or processes — that meet a defined set of criteria and constraints. Unlike the scientific method, which seeks to understand how the world is, the engineering design process seeks to change how the world works.

The most widely adopted version of the process includes these steps:

  1. Define the Problem — Identify the need or opportunity. Articulate criteria (what the solution must do) and constraints (limits on time, budget, materials, or safety).
  2. Research — Gather background information, study existing solutions, and identify relevant standards.
  3. Brainstorm Solutions — Generate a wide range of possible approaches without judgment.
  4. Select a Solution — Evaluate options against criteria and constraints; choose the most promising approach.
  5. Prototype — Build a working model or representation of the chosen solution.
  6. Test and Evaluate — Test the prototype against the criteria and constraints; measure performance.
  7. Iterate — Use test results to improve the design; repeat until the solution meets requirements.
  8. Communicate — Document the solution and present it to stakeholders.

The most critical word in that list is iterate. Real engineering design rarely moves in a straight line from problem to solution. Engineers cycle back — sometimes many times — as testing reveals weaknesses or new constraints emerge.

docs.title: Engineering vs. Science: A Direct Comparison
docs.text:
Understanding how these two frameworks differ is essential for recognizing which one to apply — and when. In practice, engineers use elements of both, but with different primary goals.

Dimension Scientific Method Engineering Design Process Primary question Why does this happen? How can we make this work? Goal Generate verified knowledge Create a solution that meets criteria Starting point Observation of a natural phenomenon Identified need or problem Key output Knowledge (publications, theories) Artifact (product, system, process) Test criteria Statistical significance Performance against criteria/constraints "Success" means Hypothesis supported or falsified Solution meets requirements Iteration purpose Refine understanding Improve design performance Community check Peer review and replication Engineering review and standards compliance

One important nuance: engineering often depends on science. When a civil engineer designs a bridge, they rely on scientific knowledge about material properties, fluid dynamics, and soil mechanics. But the design decisions — how long, how wide, what materials, what cost — are engineering judgments, not scientific conclusions.

docs.title: Diagram: Venn Diagram — Scientific Method vs. Engineering Design Process
docs.text:
Interactive Venn Diagram: Scientific Method vs. Engineering Design Process

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: science-vs-engineering-venn Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will differentiate (Bloom L4) the unique and shared features of the scientific method and engineering design process by clicking on regions of a Venn diagram to reveal categorized content.

Canvas: 800 × 420 px, responsive.

Layout: Two large overlapping circles. Left circle labeled "Scientific Method" (SteelBlue). Right circle labeled "Engineering Design Process" (DarkGoldenrod). Overlapping region is a medium gray labeled "Both".

Clickable regions (three): - Left-only region: Opens a panel listing 4 unique features of the scientific method (e.g., "Falsifiable hypothesis required," "Statistical analysis of data," "Peer-reviewed publication," "Seeks universal laws"). - Overlap region: Opens a panel listing 4 shared features (e.g., "Systematic process," "Iterative in practice," "Requires background research," "Builds on prior work"). - Right-only region: Opens a panel listing 4 unique features of the engineering design process (e.g., "Defined by criteria and constraints," "Produces a physical or digital artifact," "Optimized for performance," "Judged by standards compliance").

Panel: Appears to the right of the Venn diagram (200 px wide). Each list item is a short bullet. Clicking a different region replaces the panel content with a smooth fade.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/science-vs-engineering-venn/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 460 px height.

docs.title: Systems Thinking
docs.text:
Once you understand how engineers design solutions, the next question is: how do those solutions fit into larger organizations and industries? The answer requires systems thinking — a way of looking at problems by examining the whole system and the relationships between its parts, rather than focusing on individual components in isolation.

A system is a set of interacting or interdependent components that form a unified whole and produce behaviors that none of the components could produce alone. Systems thinking shifts the engineer's attention from "what is this part?" to "how do the parts interact, and what emerges from those interactions?"

Systems thinking is particularly powerful when:

  • A problem has multiple causes that reinforce each other
  • A solution in one area creates unexpected problems somewhere else
  • The behavior of a system changes over time in non-obvious ways
  • Stakeholders disagree about the root cause of a problem

Engineers who think in systems are better at designing solutions that do not create new problems downstream — a skill that becomes especially important when you are designing for public health, safety, or the environment.

docs.title: System Inputs and Outputs
docs.text:
Every system, no matter how complex, can be described in terms of what goes in, what happens inside, and what comes out. System inputs are the resources, information, or energy that enter the system. System outputs are the products, services, information, or waste that the system produces.

Consider a water treatment plant as a system:

Component Examples Inputs Raw water, chemicals (chlorine, alum), electricity, labor Processes Filtration, sedimentation, disinfection, pH adjustment Outputs Clean drinking water (desired), sludge (waste), treated wastewater Environment Regulatory standards, local geography, seasonal variation

Every engineering system also has a boundary — the line that separates what is inside the system from what is outside. Choosing the right boundary is a design decision: a boundary that is too narrow ignores important interactions; one that is too wide makes the analysis unmanageable.

docs.title: Feedback Loops
docs.text:
One of the most important concepts in systems thinking is the feedback loop — a mechanism in which outputs of a system circle back to influence its own inputs or internal processes. Feedback loops explain why systems often behave in surprising, non-linear ways.

There are two fundamental types of feedback loops:

Balancing (negative) feedback loops resist change and push the system back toward a target or equilibrium. They are stabilizing. A thermostat is the classic example: when room temperature rises above the set point, the system turns off the heater, which causes the temperature to fall back toward the target.

Reinforcing (positive) feedback loops amplify change and drive the system further from its starting point. They can be either virtuous (compounding growth) or vicious (accelerating collapse). Corrosion on a metal bridge is an example: exposure to moisture causes initial rusting, which makes the surface rougher and more porous, which accelerates further corrosion.

Identifying feedback loops in your practicum project's environment — financial, social, technical — will help you anticipate how your solution will behave after implementation.

docs.title: Diagram: Feedback Loop Simulator
docs.text:
Interactive Feedback Loop Simulator

Type: MicroSim sim-id: feedback-loop-simulator Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) feedback loop concepts by adjusting parameters in a simulated system and observing how balancing and reinforcing loops produce different patterns of change over time.

Canvas: 800 × 500 px, responsive.

System simulated: A simplified project budget system. The state variable is "Budget Remaining" (starts at $10,000). Two sliders control the system: - Spending Rate (slider: $100–$1,000 per week) — a drain on budget - Correction Strength (slider: 0–1.0) — how aggressively a manager reduces spending when budget falls below a warning threshold ($3,000)

Left panel (controls, 250 px): - Spending Rate slider with label and current value - Correction Strength slider with label and current value - Loop Type radio buttons: "Balancing Only," "Reinforcing Only," "Both" - Reset button

Right panel (visualization, 550 px): A line chart (Chart.js embedded in p5 canvas or separate div) showing Budget Remaining over 52 weeks. The x-axis is Week (0–52); the y-axis is Budget ($0–$12,000). A horizontal dashed red line marks the Warning Threshold at $3,000.

When "Balancing Only" is selected: the line stabilizes around the threshold as the correction kicks in (S-curve behavior). When "Reinforcing Only" is selected: spending accelerates as budget falls (the project manager panics and spends more trying to fix problems), producing a rapid drop to zero. When "Both" is selected: the system oscillates.

Interaction: Sliders update the simulation in real time. Hovering over any point on the line reveals a tooltip with the exact week and budget value. Clicking on the chart pauses the animation; clicking again resumes.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/feedback-loop-simulator/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 560 px height.

docs.title: Subsystem Interactions
docs.text:
Large engineering systems are rarely designed as single monolithic units. Instead, they are broken into subsystems — smaller, semi-independent components that each perform a specific function and interact with each other through defined interfaces.

Subsystem interactions are the connections, data flows, energy transfers, and material flows that pass between subsystems. Understanding how subsystems interact is essential because:

  • A change in one subsystem can propagate through interfaces and affect others unexpectedly
  • The performance of the overall system depends on how well subsystems communicate and cooperate
  • Failures at the interface between subsystems are among the most common causes of engineering disasters

Consider an electric vehicle (EV) as a system of interacting subsystems:

Subsystem Function Key Interfaces Battery Pack Store electrical energy Electrical connection to motor controller Motor Controller Regulate power delivery Electrical (battery), mechanical (motor), data (sensors) Drive Motor Convert electrical energy to motion Mechanical connection to drivetrain Thermal Management Maintain battery temperature Heat exchanger, coolant loop, sensors Regenerative Braking Recover kinetic energy as electricity Mechanical (brakes), electrical (battery charging circuit) Driver Interface Communicate state to driver Data bus, display, controls

Notice that the thermal management subsystem interacts with the battery pack, the motor, and the environment — a change in ambient temperature affects multiple subsystems simultaneously. This web of interactions is exactly what systems thinking is designed to help you navigate.

docs.title: Applying These Frameworks to the Scavenger Hunt
docs.text:
Your Engineering Industry Scavenger Hunt asks you to investigate a real engineering industry or organization and find evidence of the frameworks introduced in this chapter. As you research your assigned industry, use these questions as a guide:

  • Where does this industry use the scientific method? (R&D labs, testing, regulatory submissions)
  • Where does it use the engineering design process? (Product development, construction, system upgrades)
  • What are the major inputs and outputs of this industry as a system?
  • What feedback loops can you identify? (Quality control, market signals, regulatory responses)
  • What are the major subsystems and how do they interact?

Documenting your answers — with real examples from your chosen industry — will prepare you to write a sharp Engineering Design Process vs. Scientific Method Comparison and to present your findings to the class.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
The two foundational frameworks introduced in this chapter will appear in every engineering course you take from here forward:

  • The scientific method is a process for generating knowledge about the natural world through observation, hypothesis testing, and evidence-based reasoning.
  • A hypothesis must be falsifiable and is tested against a null hypothesis using controlled experiments and statistical analysis.
  • The engineering design process is a systematic, iterative approach to creating solutions that meet defined criteria and constraints — it produces artifacts, not knowledge.
  • Engineering and science are complementary: engineering relies on scientific knowledge, but its goal is problem-solving rather than knowledge generation.
  • Systems thinking views problems through the lens of interacting components, boundaries, inputs, outputs, and emergent behavior.
  • System inputs and outputs define what enters and leaves a system; identifying them is the first step in analyzing any engineering organization.
  • Feedback loops — balancing (stabilizing) and reinforcing (amplifying) — explain how systems respond to change over time.
  • Subsystem interactions are the interfaces between semi-independent components; failures at those interfaces are a major source of engineering problems.

Carry these frameworks into every chapter ahead. When you encounter a new engineering challenge, your first instinct should be to ask: Am I doing science or engineering? And what system am I working within?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter focuses on building a professional work portfolio — the document that shows employers and colleges not just what you have done, but how you think and what you are capable of. Students learn what a strong engineering portfolio contains, gather and organize their existing work, and identify gaps to fill before graduation.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text: Portfolio Development — CTE-43, CTE-21

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 2 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Work Portfolio
  2. Portfolio Components

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Getting hired in engineering requires more than technical knowledge. Employers and college admissions officers evaluate candidates on how clearly they communicate their skills and what evidence they can provide that those skills are real. A portfolio is that evidence — it is the difference between telling someone you can engineer something and showing them.

Everything you create in this chapter has a second purpose beyond the assignment: it is the beginning of a professional record you will carry into college, internships, and your career. The habit of documenting your work — what you built, what you learned, what you would do differently — is one of the most underrated skills in engineering.

docs.title: Your Work Portfolio
docs.text:
A work portfolio is a curated collection of materials that demonstrates your skills, experiences, and growth as an engineering student. Unlike a resume, which summarizes your background on a single page, a portfolio shows your work rather than just describing it. Employers and admissions officers use portfolios to see how you think, not just what titles you have held.

Portfolios exist in two formats. A physical portfolio is a binder or folder containing printed documents, photos, and artifacts. A digital portfolio is a website, PDF, or platform-hosted collection — for example, Google Sites, Wix, or Adobe Portfolio — that can be shared via a link. Digital portfolios are increasingly preferred in engineering because they are easy to update, shareable, and can include video and interactive content.

The most effective portfolios are organized around outcomes — what you achieved — rather than activities alone. "Designed and tested three bridge prototypes that held an average of 42 lbs" is stronger than "Participated in a bridge project." The distinction matters: outcomes show impact, activities show presence.

docs.title: Portfolio Components
docs.text:
Portfolio components are the specific types of materials that go inside a work portfolio. Including a variety of component types demonstrates breadth of experience and professional intentionality. The following categories are standard for engineering students:

Component What It Shows Example Personal statement Goals, values, and professional identity "Why I want to be a civil engineer" Resume Summary of education, skills, experience One-page formatted resume Project descriptions Problem-solving process and outcomes Design brief + photos of prototype Academic work samples Technical depth and writing ability Lab report, CAD drawings, calculations Certifications and awards Recognized achievements OSHA 10 certificate, robotics competition award Reflections Growth mindset and self-awareness "What I learned from a failed design" Letters of recommendation External validation of your skills Teacher or mentor letter Contact information Professional reachability Email, LinkedIn, portfolio URL

Not every student will have all of these components right now — and that is fine. The goal at this stage is to gather what you have and identify the gaps you want to fill before graduation. A portfolio is never finished; it grows with you.

docs.title: What Makes a Component Portfolio-Worthy?
docs.text:
Before adding something to your portfolio, apply a three-question test:

  1. Does it demonstrate a skill? The piece should show something you can do, not just something you participated in.
  2. Does it show your thinking? The best portfolio entries include not just the final product, but some evidence of the process — a rough sketch, a design iteration, a reflection on what changed and why.
  3. Is it your best work? Portfolios are curated, not comprehensive. Five excellent pieces are more powerful than fifteen mediocre ones.

If a piece passes all three questions, it belongs. If it fails any one of them, either improve it first or leave it out.

docs.title: Diagram: Portfolio Component Checklist Builder
docs.text:
Interactive Portfolio Component Checklist Builder

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: portfolio-checklist-builder Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will evaluate (Bloom L5) the completeness of their current portfolio by checking off components they have, identifying gaps, and receiving a readiness score.

Canvas: 800 × 520 px, responsive to window resize.

Left panel (checklist — 320 px): Each portfolio component appears as a labeled checkbox row. When checked, the row turns green. A progress bar at the bottom tracks the number of components checked out of the total.

Right panel (readiness gauge — 480 px): A semicircular gauge (like a speedometer) labeled "Portfolio Readiness." The needle moves from 0% to 100% as components are checked. Three zones are color-coded:

  • 0–40%: Red — "Getting started"
  • 41–70%: Gold — "Making progress"
  • 71–100%: Green — "Strong portfolio"

Below the gauge, a dynamic text area lists the unchecked components as "Next steps to strengthen your portfolio."

Interaction: Clicking any checkbox immediately updates the gauge and the next-steps list. Hovering over a component name shows a one-sentence tooltip explaining why that component matters to employers.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/portfolio-checklist-builder/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Organizing Your Portfolio
docs.text:
A strong portfolio is not just a collection — it is a narrative. The reader should be able to move through your portfolio and understand your growth as an engineering student from beginning to end. Two organizational strategies work well:

  • Chronological order — arrange pieces from oldest to most recent; this makes your growth visible over time
  • Project-centered order — group everything related to one project together; this is better when a single project was especially complex or significant

For most high school students, a hybrid approach works best: lead with your personal statement and resume, then organize the middle section by project, and close with a reflection on what you have learned across the full sequence.

Whatever order you choose, every piece needs a brief label — a title, date, course or context, and one sentence explaining why you included it. These labels transform a scrapbook into a portfolio.

docs.title: Digital Portfolio Platforms
docs.text:
Several free platforms are well-suited to engineering student portfolios. The table below compares them on the features that matter most for this assignment.

Platform Ease of Setup Custom URL Embeds Video/Media Best For Google Sites Very easy With Google Workspace Yes Students already using Google tools Wix Easy With free plan (subdomain) Yes More design control Adobe Portfolio Moderate Yes (with Creative Cloud) Yes Students with design/visual work PDF/Document Very easy N/A (shared as file) No Simple portfolios, physical printing GitHub Pages Requires coding Yes Yes Students with coding experience

For this course, any format that can be shared digitally is acceptable. The content and curation matter far more than the platform.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
This chapter established what a work portfolio is and how to build one that demonstrates your engineering skills and growth:

  • A work portfolio curates your best work to show skills rather than just claim them — it complements your resume with evidence.
  • Portfolio components span eight standard categories; a strong portfolio includes materials from most of them, chosen to pass the three-question test (demonstrates a skill, shows thinking, represents your best work).
  • Organization matters: arrange your portfolio as a narrative of growth, with labeled entries that explain why each piece was included.
  • Digital portfolios are preferred in engineering; choose a platform based on what you already use and what your work looks like.

In the next chapter, you will take these portfolio materials into the job application process — using your portfolio to support a resume, cover letter, and ultimately a mock interview.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter prepares students to enter the engineering job market. Building on the portfolio developed in Chapter 3, students write a professional resume and targeted cover letter, learn how the engineering job application process works, explore internship opportunities, and develop the interview skills needed to perform well in a realistic mock interview setting.

docs.title: Assignments
docs.text:
• Cover Letter — CTE-44, CTE-43, CTE-21
  • Resume — CTE-44, CTE-43, CTE-21
  • Professional Mock Interview Event — CTE-65

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 6 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Resume Writing
  2. Cover Letter Writing
  3. Job Application Process
  4. Engineering Internships
  5. Interview Skills
  6. Mock Interview Practice

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration
  • Chapter 3: Portfolio Development

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Your portfolio shows what you can do. Now you need documents and skills that get you in the room to talk about it. This chapter covers the three application tools every engineering candidate needs — a resume, a cover letter, and the ability to interview well — and puts them together in a mock interview that mirrors a real hiring event.

These skills are not just for after graduation. Internship applications, college supplemental materials, scholarship applications, and scholarship interviews all use the same format. Building these competencies now, while you have time to practice and refine, is one of the highest-leverage things you can do in this course.

docs.title: Resume Writing
docs.text:
A resume is a one-page professional document that summarizes your education, skills, work experience, and relevant achievements for a specific audience — typically an employer or college admissions officer. The word "résumé" comes from French meaning "summary," which captures exactly what it is: a tight, curated snapshot of your professional story.

Before examining the structure, two vocabulary terms need clear definitions. Quantifiable results are achievements expressed with numbers ("increased efficiency by 20%," "designed a structure that held 47 lbs"). Action verbs are strong, specific verbs that begin bullet points to show what you did rather than what you were responsible for — words like designed, analyzed, built, tested, optimized, and presented.

The standard engineering resume is organized into these sections:

  • Header — Name, phone, email, and portfolio or LinkedIn URL (no home address required)
  • Education — School name, expected graduation year, GPA if 3.5 or above, relevant coursework
  • Skills — Technical skills (CAD software, programming languages, lab equipment) and professional skills
  • Experience — Internships, jobs, or significant volunteer roles; each entry has 2–4 bullet points beginning with action verbs
  • Projects — Class or personal engineering projects (often the most important section for students with limited work experience)
  • Awards and Activities — Competitions, student organizations, certifications

A one-page rule applies to students and early-career engineers. Hiring managers spend an average of 6–7 seconds scanning a resume before deciding whether to read it closely. White space, consistent formatting, and a clean 11–12 pt font (Calibri, Arial, or Garamond) are not cosmetic concerns — they are functional ones that signal professional judgment.

Resume Mistake Why It Hurts Using "responsible for" instead of action verbs Sounds passive; doesn't show what you actually did Missing quantifiable results Claims aren't credible without supporting numbers Inconsistent formatting (mixed fonts, sizes) Signals lack of attention to detail Including a photo or personal info (age, gender) Unprofessional in the U.S. context; creates legal risk Generic objective statement Wastes prime space; every applicant wants "a challenging position" More than one page (for students) Suggests poor judgment about what matters

docs.title: Diagram: Resume Section Builder
docs.text:
Interactive Resume Section Builder

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: resume-section-builder Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) resume writing conventions by assembling a sample resume section using a drag-and-drop interface and receiving feedback on action verb strength and quantification.

Canvas: 900 × 560 px, responsive.

Left panel — Element bank (280 px): A scrollable list of 12 sample bullet points, some written with strong action verbs and quantifiable results, some written weakly (passive voice, vague language). Each is draggable.

Center panel — Resume preview (400 px): A simplified resume template showing an Experience section with three empty bullet-point slots. Students drag bullet points from the element bank into the slots.

Right panel — Feedback (220 px): As bullets are dropped in, each is evaluated on two criteria: (1) does it begin with an action verb? (2) does it contain a number or measurable result? A green checkmark or red X appears for each criterion, with a one-line suggestion for improvement on red items.

"Improve It" mode: Clicking a bullet in the resume preview opens an editable text field so students can rewrite the bullet and re-check it.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/resume-section-builder/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 620 px height.

docs.title: Cover Letter Writing
docs.text:
A cover letter is a one-page business letter submitted alongside a resume. Its purpose is not to repeat the resume — it is to explain why you are applying to this specific position and why you are the right fit. A cover letter gives you space to tell a story that bullet points cannot.

The three-paragraph structure works for most engineering cover letters:

  1. Opening paragraph — State the specific position you are applying for, how you learned about it, and one sentence that immediately signals your strongest qualification. Do not start with "My name is..." — the reader already knows that from the header.
  2. Body paragraph — Connect two or three of your specific experiences (drawn from your resume or portfolio) to the requirements listed in the job posting. Use concrete examples, not generalities.
  3. Closing paragraph — Thank the reader for their time, express enthusiasm, and include a specific call to action: "I would welcome the opportunity to discuss how my experience in structural analysis could contribute to your team."

The most common cover letter mistake is writing the same generic letter for every application. Employers notice immediately when a letter is not customized. Look at the job posting carefully: reuse the company's own vocabulary, reference a specific project or value from their website, and address the letter to a named person whenever you can find one.

Element Strong Example Weak Example Opening hook "Your firm's recent work on the Route 7 bridge rehabilitation is exactly the kind of infrastructure challenge I want to contribute to." "I am writing to apply for the engineering internship position." Specific connection "In my Engineering III course, I designed a load-bearing structure that reduced material cost by 18% through iterative prototyping." "I have strong engineering skills and work well in teams." Call to action "I would welcome the chance to discuss how my CAD and project management experience could support your summer team." "I look forward to hearing from you."

docs.title: The Job Application Process
docs.text:
The job application process is the sequence of steps between identifying a job opening and receiving an employment offer. Understanding each step reduces anxiety and increases the chance of success. For engineering internships and entry-level positions, the typical sequence is:

  1. Job search — Identify openings through job boards (LinkedIn, Indeed, Handshake), company websites, professional society job boards (ASCE, ASME, IEEE), and networking.
  2. Tailoring materials — Customize your resume and cover letter for each position; adjust the skills section to match keywords in the job description.
  3. Submission — Apply through the company's online portal; upload resume and cover letter in PDF format; complete any online screening questions.
  4. ATS screening — Many companies use Applicant Tracking System software to scan applications for keywords before a human reads them. Using exact language from the job posting helps your application pass this filter.
  5. Phone or video screening — A 15–30 minute call with a recruiter to verify basic qualifications and interest.
  6. Technical interview or assessment — Some engineering employers assign a take-home problem or a coding/design challenge.
  7. Panel or on-site interview — One or more in-person or video interviews with hiring managers and team members.
  8. Offer — If selected, you receive a formal offer letter with compensation, start date, and terms.

docs.title: Diagram: Job Application Process Flowchart
docs.text:
Interactive Job Application Process Flowchart

Type: workflow diagram sim-id: job-application-flowchart Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will recall (Bloom L1) the correct sequence of the job application process and apply (Bloom L3) it by identifying where in the process a given scenario falls.

Canvas: 800 × 520 px, responsive.

Layout: Eight rectangular nodes arranged in a vertical flowchart with connecting arrows. Each node is labeled with a step name and numbered. Nodes are color-coded by phase: blue (search/prep), gold (submission), green (interviews), teal (offer).

Interaction: Clicking any node opens a side panel (300 px wide) showing: step name, a 2–3 sentence description, and one student tip for succeeding at this step.

"Where am I?" quiz mode: A button at the bottom labeled "Test Yourself" presents a brief scenario sentence (e.g., "You just received an email from a recruiter asking for a 20-minute call — what step are you in?") and the student clicks the correct node. The node flashes green for correct or red for incorrect, with a brief explanation.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/job-application-flowchart/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Engineering Internships
docs.text:
An engineering internship is a structured, paid work experience — typically 10–12 weeks during the summer — in which a student works at an engineering firm, company, or government agency under the supervision of licensed engineers. Internships are one of the highest-impact steps you can take as an engineering student.

The four primary benefits of an internship are:

  • Real project experience that strengthens your resume and gives your portfolio new, professional-context entries
  • Field confirmation — internships confirm (or challenge) your interest in a specialization before you commit to a career path
  • Professional network at a time when connections are especially valuable for recommendations and future job searches
  • Employment pipeline — according to the National Association of Colleges and Employers (NACE), approximately 70% of interns who perform well receive a full-time offer from the same employer

Most engineering internships are available to students who have completed at least their freshman year of college, but some companies offer high school shadowing and pre-college programs. Virginia's GO Virginia initiative and programs like NYLF Engineering are worth researching now.

When comparing internship opportunities, evaluate four factors beyond the hourly wage:

Factor Questions to Ask Mentorship quality Will I work directly with engineers, or mainly do administrative tasks? Project ownership Will I contribute to a real deliverable, or just observe? Location and logistics Remote, in-person, or hybrid? Is housing provided or stipended? Field alignment Does this company's work match my engineering specialization?

docs.title: Interview Skills
docs.text:
Interview skills are the communication and professional behaviors that allow a candidate to effectively present their qualifications, experience, and personality during a hiring conversation. Unlike resumes and cover letters — which you prepare in advance — interviews require real-time performance under some pressure.

The most widely used framework for answering behavioral interview questions is the STAR method. Each letter stands for one component of a structured answer:

  • Situation — The context or background of a specific past event
  • Task — What you were responsible for in that situation
  • Action — The specific steps you took
  • Result — What happened because of those steps, quantified when possible

A behavioral interview question asks you to describe a real experience: "Tell me about a time you faced a technical challenge you didn't know how to solve." A well-structured STAR answer sounds like: "In my Engineering III class (Situation), my team's bridge design failed the first load test and we had less than a week to redesign it (Task). I analyzed the failure points and proposed switching from a truss to an arch design based on overnight research (Action). Our redesigned bridge held 50% more weight than the original (Result)."

Professional behavior during an interview is evaluated before you say a word. The table below covers the non-verbal standards that interviewers notice immediately.

Behavior Standard Arrival time 5–10 minutes early; never late Attire Business casual minimum; business professional preferred Handshake and eye contact Firm, brief; maintain comfortable eye contact Phone Silent and completely out of sight for the entire interview Note-taking Bringing a notepad signals engagement and is entirely appropriate Questions for the interviewer Always have 2–3 thoughtful questions prepared for the end

docs.title: Mock Interview Practice
docs.text:
Mock interview practice is a rehearsal exercise in which a student simulates a real job interview with a teacher, classmate, or community professional serving as the interviewer. Research on interview performance consistently shows that students who practice aloud — not just mentally — perform significantly better than those who only think through their answers.

Your Professional Mock Interview Event will be structured like a real interview: business dress, formal introductions, a set of behavioral and technical questions, and written feedback from the interviewer. Treat this exactly as you would treat a real opportunity.

Preparation steps before your mock interview:

  1. Research the "company" — Read the job description carefully; understand what the role requires and what the organization values.
  2. Prepare STAR stories — Write out three to five experiences from your resume or portfolio in STAR format. Each story should be flexible enough to answer multiple question types.
  3. Practice aloud — Speak your answers out loud, not just in your head. Most answers should run 90 seconds to 2 minutes.
  4. Prepare your questions — Write two or three genuine questions about the role or the team. Generic questions ("What is the salary?") signal poor preparation.
  5. Dress and logistics — Lay out your attire the night before; bring a printed copy of your resume and, if applicable, your portfolio.

After the mock interview, the written feedback you receive is as valuable as the rehearsal itself. Review every comment, identify one or two specific behaviors to change, and carry those adjustments forward into every future interview.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
This chapter built the employment preparation toolkit that every engineering candidate needs — documents that open doors and skills that close them:

  • A resume is a one-page, action-verb-driven summary of your qualifications. Quantify your results, keep it to one page, and format it for a 6-second scan.
  • A cover letter explains why you want this specific opportunity and connects your experience to the employer's stated needs — it is never a copy of your resume in paragraph form.
  • The job application process follows a defined sequence from search to offer; understanding each step — including ATS screening — removes surprises and lets you prepare strategically.
  • Engineering internships are among the most valuable experiences available; evaluate them on mentorship quality and project ownership, not just pay.
  • Interview skills are practiced, not innate. The STAR method gives you a reliable structure for any behavioral question.
  • Mock interview practice is the single most effective preparation you can do before a real interview — take the event seriously and act on the feedback.

Your resume, cover letter, and portfolio all feed each other: a strong portfolio makes your resume more credible, a sharp resume makes the cover letter easier to write, and strong application documents give you confidence when the interview starts.

docs.title: Chapter 5 - Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP
Chapter 5: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP
Summary
docs.text: This is the launch chapter for the practicum project. Students identify a real engineering need or opportunity, define their challenge, and begin applying project management thinking to their solution. Woven throughout is an introduction to the ethical and legal responsibilities engineers carry — including professional ethics, intellectual property rights, and the obligations engineers have to the public — framed as essential context for making good design decisions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration
  • Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Every engineering project begins not with a solution, but with a problem. Before you can design anything, you need to clearly understand what challenge you are trying to address, who is affected by it, and what limitations your solution must work within. This chapter walks you through that process — from noticing a problem in the world around you to writing a focused engineering challenge definition that will anchor your practicum project.

But defining a good problem is only part of what engineers do at the start of a project. Engineers also carry significant responsibilities: to their clients, to the public, and to the profession itself. This chapter introduces the ethical codes that govern engineering practice and the intellectual property laws that protect creative and technical work. Understanding both sets of responsibilities will help you make better decisions as you design and document your project.

docs.title: Problem Identification
docs.text:
Problem identification is the process of recognizing that a gap exists between how things currently work and how they could or should work. In engineering, a problem is not simply an annoyance — it is an observable condition that causes harm, inefficiency, or unmet need for a specific group of people or a system.

Engineers find problems by observing the world carefully, listening to the people who experience a gap, and asking questions like:

  • Who is affected, and how often?
  • What is the cost — in time, money, safety, or quality of life — of leaving this problem unsolved?
  • Has someone already tried to solve it? Why did that approach fall short?

Not every frustration is a viable engineering problem. A problem worth engineering has a measurable impact, a definable scope, and at least the possibility of a designed solution.

docs.title: Needs Assessment
docs.text:
Once you have identified a candidate problem, the next step is a needs assessment — a structured investigation that helps you confirm the problem is real, understand it fully, and determine whether it is worth solving. A needs assessment typically involves four activities:

  1. Stakeholder interviews — talking directly to people who experience the problem
  2. Data collection — gathering numbers that quantify the impact (how many people, how often, what cost)
  3. Root cause analysis — asking "why does this problem exist?" repeatedly until you reach the underlying cause rather than a surface symptom
  4. Literature review — researching whether existing solutions exist and why they may be insufficient

The output of a needs assessment is a clear statement of the gap: what is happening now, what should be happening, and what is preventing the improvement.

docs.title: Opportunity Recognition
docs.text:
Problems and opportunities are two sides of the same coin. Opportunity recognition is the skill of reframing a problem as a specific, actionable opportunity for a designed solution. This shift in framing matters because it moves your thinking from what is wrong to what could be built.

Compare these two framings of the same situation:

Problem Frame Opportunity Frame "Students at our school waste time looking for open parking spaces." "An opportunity exists to design a low-cost sensor system that displays real-time parking availability on a map." "Elderly residents in this neighborhood can't read food labels easily." "An opportunity exists to develop a smartphone app that reads and enlarges nutrition information using the phone's camera." "The school's garden irrigation wastes water during rainy weeks." "An opportunity exists to build an automated irrigation controller that checks a weather API before opening the valves."

Notice that the opportunity frame names a specific intervention and a specific beneficiary. This specificity is what makes the opportunity actionable.

docs.title: Engineering Challenge Definition
docs.text:
An engineering challenge definition (sometimes called a problem statement) is a concise, written statement that captures the problem, the stakeholders affected, the measurable impact, and the constraints and goals for a potential solution. It is the single most important document you will write at the start of your project, because every design decision you make later should trace back to it.

A well-written engineering challenge definition has four components:

  1. The situation — a one-sentence description of the current state and who is affected
  2. The need — a clear statement of what is missing or insufficient
  3. The impact — a quantified description of the cost of the problem
  4. The goal — what a successful solution would accomplish, expressed in measurable terms

Example:

Students at [School Name] spend an average of 8 minutes per day searching for open parking spots during peak arrival hours, resulting in tardiness for approximately 30 students per week. A successful solution would reduce average search time to under 2 minutes without requiring students to download an app or access the school's Wi-Fi network.

This statement is specific, measurable, and does not prescribe a particular solution — it leaves the design space open.

docs.title: Diagram: Problem Statement Builder
docs.text:
Interactive Problem Statement Builder

Canvas: 820 × 560 px, responsive to window resize.

The Impact (quantify the cost in time, money, safety, or quality — include a number) 4. The Goal (what a successful solution accomplishes, in measurable terms)

docs.title: Constraints and Criteria
docs.text:
Before brainstorming solutions, engineers define the boundaries within which any solution must operate. These boundaries take two forms: constraints and criteria.

A constraint is a non-negotiable limit — a condition that any valid solution must satisfy. Constraints come from physics, safety regulations, available materials, time, budget, or the needs of stakeholders. If a solution violates a constraint, it is not a viable solution regardless of its other merits.

A criterion (plural: criteria) is a desired quality — a goal the solution should achieve as much as possible. Unlike constraints, criteria can be partially satisfied. They are used to compare solutions and choose between alternatives.

Type Definition Example Constraint Must be satisfied (binary — yes or no) Solution must cost less than $200 to build Constraint Must be satisfied Solution must not require a WiFi connection Criterion Should be maximized or optimized Solution should be as lightweight as possible Criterion Should be maximized or optimized Solution should be usable by people of all ages

Listing your constraints and criteria before you brainstorm keeps your design process honest — it prevents you from falling in love with an idea that cannot actually work.

docs.title: Brainstorming Solutions
docs.text:
Brainstorming is a structured ideation technique for generating a large number of candidate solutions before evaluating any of them. The critical rule of productive brainstorming is deferred judgment: during the generative phase, no idea is criticized or eliminated. Premature evaluation kills creative thinking.

Effective brainstorming techniques for engineering include:

  • Mind mapping — starting with the engineering challenge at the center and branching outward into different solution categories
  • SCAMPER — systematically asking whether existing solutions can be Substituted, Combined, Adapted, Modified, Put to other uses, Eliminated, or Reversed
  • Crazy eights — sketching eight rough solution concepts in eight minutes to push past obvious first ideas
  • Biomimicry prompting — asking "how does nature solve a similar problem?"

After the generative phase, you evaluate candidates against your constraints and criteria — eliminating those that violate constraints and ranking the rest by how well they satisfy the criteria. The goal is to narrow to two or three strong candidates worth developing further.

docs.title: Project Management
docs.text:
Project management is the discipline of planning, organizing, and controlling the work required to complete a defined goal within a given time and budget. For your practicum, project management is not optional — without it, well-intentioned projects stall, run over budget, and miss deadlines.

The five core functions of project management are:

  1. Scope definition — specifying exactly what the project will and will not include
  2. Scheduling — breaking the work into tasks and assigning start and end dates
  3. Resource planning — identifying what materials, tools, and time are needed
  4. Risk management — anticipating what could go wrong and planning responses
  5. Communication — keeping stakeholders informed of progress, changes, and decisions

You will build detailed project management artifacts — a work breakdown structure, a Gantt chart, and a budget — in Chapters 6 and 7. Here, the goal is to understand project management as a mindset: engineering success is not just about a good idea; it is about executing that idea systematically.

docs.title: Engineering Ethics and the Code of Ethics
docs.text:
Engineering ethics is the branch of applied ethics that examines the moral principles and professional values that should guide engineers in their work. Because engineers design systems and structures that affect public safety, the environment, and the quality of daily life, the ethical stakes of engineering decisions are unusually high.

Most major engineering professional societies publish a formal Code of Ethics — a written set of principles that members are expected to uphold. While the specific language varies by organization (NSPE, ASCE, IEEE, ASME all publish their own), every engineering code of ethics shares a common core:

  • Hold the safety, health, and welfare of the public as the paramount concern
  • Be honest and impartial in professional reports, statements, and testimony
  • Act with competence — only undertake work within your area of expertise
  • Avoid conflicts of interest or disclose them fully when they cannot be avoided
  • Protect confidential information entrusted by clients or employers

These codes are not merely aspirational. Licensed Professional Engineers who violate them can face suspension or revocation of their license by the state engineering board.

docs.title: Professional Responsibility and Public Safety
docs.text:
Professional responsibility refers to an engineer's obligation to perform their work competently, honestly, and with regard for the consequences of their designs on others. This responsibility is especially weighty because engineers often design systems that members of the public use without any ability to evaluate their safety independently.

Public safety in engineering is the principle that no commercial, competitive, or personal interest justifies knowingly placing the public at risk. Engineering history contains sobering examples of what happens when this principle is violated or ignored:

  • The Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940) — resonance effects that engineers understood theoretically were not adequately tested at scale.
  • The Challenger Space Shuttle disaster (1986) — engineers raised O-ring concerns before launch; organizational pressure contributed to the decision to proceed.
  • The Flint, Michigan water crisis (2014–2019) — cost-cutting decisions led to lead contamination of the public water supply, affecting thousands of children.

These cases are studied not to assign blame decades later, but because they reveal the conditions under which ethical failures occur — and the systematic approaches that can prevent them.

docs.title: Ethical Decision Making
docs.text:
Ethical decision making in engineering is the process of recognizing an ethical dimension in a situation, identifying the stakeholders and values involved, evaluating available options against ethical principles, and choosing a course of action that can be professionally defended.

A widely used framework for engineering ethics cases is the following four-step process:

  1. Recognize the ethical issue — Is there a potential for harm? Whose interests are in conflict?
  2. Gather the facts — What do you know? What do you need to find out? Who has relevant expertise?
  3. Evaluate the options — Which option best upholds the core engineering values? What are the consequences of each option for each stakeholder?
  4. Act and reflect — Choose the most defensible option, document your reasoning, and reflect on the outcome.

docs.title: Personal vs. Professional Ethics
docs.text:
A distinction that surprises many students is that personal ethics and professional ethics are not always the same. Personal ethics refers to the values and moral principles a person holds as an individual — shaped by family, culture, religion, and experience. Professional ethics refers to the standards of conduct defined by the profession itself, independent of any individual's personal beliefs.

This distinction matters in engineering because you may encounter situations where your personal preferences differ from what your professional code requires:

  • Personal belief: "I trust my client's word." Professional obligation: An engineer must verify safety-critical claims independently.
  • Personal preference: "I don't want to make trouble by raising this concern." Professional obligation: If you observe a safety hazard, you are obligated to report it.

The professional code is not a replacement for personal ethics — it is a floor, a minimum standard that all engineers must meet regardless of their individual values. Most engineers find that their personal and professional ethics align closely, but when they conflict, the professional code governs professional conduct.

docs.title: Conflict of Interest
docs.text:
A conflict of interest exists when an engineer's personal, financial, or other interests could compromise — or appear to compromise — their professional judgment. Conflicts of interest are among the most common ethical challenges in engineering practice.

Examples of conflicts of interest in engineering:

  • An engineer who owns stock in a materials supplier recommends that supplier's products on a client project without disclosing the ownership.
  • An engineer reviewing a competitor's design for a government contract has a personal friendship with the competitor's lead engineer.
  • An engineer accepts a gift from a vendor who wants to be included in an upcoming project specification.

The standard response to a conflict of interest is disclosure — informing all affected parties and, if the conflict cannot be adequately managed, recusing yourself from the decision. The NSPE Code of Ethics states that engineers shall "act in such a manner as to uphold and enhance the honor, integrity, and dignity of the engineering profession" — which requires proactively managing conflicts even when disclosure is uncomfortable.

docs.title: What Is Intellectual Property?
docs.text:
Intellectual property (IP) refers to creations of the mind — inventions, designs, artistic works, symbols, names, and trade information — that are protected by law. The purpose of IP law is to encourage innovation by giving creators exclusive rights to benefit from their work for a defined period, while ultimately releasing the work into the public domain for everyone's benefit.

For engineers, IP is not an abstract legal concept — it is a practical reality that shapes what you can design, what you can document publicly, and what protections you can seek for your own innovations. Understanding the four main categories of IP protection is essential before you begin designing and documenting your practicum project.

docs.title: Patents
docs.text:
A patent is a legal right granted by the federal government (via the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office, USPTO) that gives the inventor the exclusive right to make, use, and sell an invention for a limited period — typically 20 years from the filing date. In exchange for this exclusive right, the inventor must publicly disclose exactly how the invention works.

The three types of patents most relevant to engineering are:

  • Utility patents — protect new and useful processes, machines, articles of manufacture, or compositions of matter (the most common type for engineering inventions)
  • Design patents — protect the ornamental or aesthetic design of a functional item (14 years)
  • Plant patents — protect distinct and new plant varieties (less common in engineering)

For an invention to be patentable, it must be novel (not previously disclosed), non-obvious (not an obvious improvement to someone skilled in the field), and useful. The patent application process is complex and typically requires a registered patent attorney or agent.

docs.title: Copyrights
docs.text:
A copyright is a form of IP protection that automatically applies to original creative works the moment they are created and fixed in a tangible form — no registration required, though registration with the U.S. Copyright Office provides legal advantages. Copyright protects the expression of an idea, not the idea itself.

In engineering contexts, copyright protects:

  • Technical reports, white papers, and written documentation
  • Engineering drawings and schematics
  • Software source code
  • Photographs and illustrations used in project materials

Copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 70 years (for individual authors). For corporate or "work for hire" authorship, the term is 95 years from publication or 120 years from creation, whichever is shorter.

Important distinction: Copyright does not protect the underlying engineering concepts or methods described in a document. To protect an invention itself, a patent is required.

docs.title: Trademarks
docs.text:
A trademark is a word, phrase, logo, symbol, or combination thereof that identifies the source of a product or service and distinguishes it from competitors. Trademarks are primarily relevant to commercial engineering products rather than engineering processes.

Examples of trademarks in engineering-adjacent contexts:

  • The LEGO® name and brick design
  • The Intel® logo and the "Intel Inside" phrase
  • The caterpillar tracks design associated with Caterpillar Inc.

Trademark rights can last indefinitely as long as the mark remains in use and is actively enforced. Marks registered with the USPTO may use the ® symbol; unregistered marks that have been used in commerce may use the ™ symbol.

docs.title: Trade Secrets
docs.text:
A trade secret is confidential business information that provides a competitive advantage and is protected by keeping it secret — there is no registration process, and protection lasts as long as the information remains confidential. The Coca-Cola formula and Google's search algorithm are famous examples.

In engineering practice, trade secrets commonly include:

  • Proprietary manufacturing processes
  • Customer lists and pricing strategies
  • Internal engineering specifications not disclosed in patents
  • Software algorithms and source code (when kept confidential rather than patented or copyrighted)

The legal protection for trade secrets requires that the owner take "reasonable measures" to keep the information secret — such as non-disclosure agreements (NDAs), restricted access to documents, and secure storage of digital files.

docs.title: Diagram: IP Type Selector
docs.text:
Interactive IP Protection Type Selector

Canvas: 820 × 560 px, responsive.

  • "Is this an invention or process?" → Yes: Patent branch / No: continue

Reset button returns to the start of the flowchart.

docs.title: IP Protection and Enforcement
docs.text:
Obtaining IP protection is one step; enforcing that protection is another. IP protection and enforcement refers to the legal mechanisms and actions available to the holder of an IP right when that right is violated.

Key enforcement mechanisms include:

  • Civil litigation — filing a lawsuit in federal court seeking an injunction (stop order) and monetary damages
  • Cease and desist letters — a written demand to stop infringing activity, often the first step before litigation
  • International Trade Commission (ITC) proceedings — used to block importation of infringing goods at the U.S. border
  • DMCA takedowns — used to remove infringing digital content under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act

Enforcement is expensive and time-consuming, which is why large corporations employ legal departments dedicated to IP matters. For individual inventors and small engineering firms, the cost of enforcement can sometimes exceed the value of the protected innovation — a practical reality that influences how engineers and companies decide what to protect and how.

docs.title: IP Infringement
docs.text:
IP infringement occurs when someone makes, uses, sells, or distributes protected IP without permission from the rights holder. The consequences of infringement can include civil damages (often calculated as the infringer's profits or the rights holder's lost profits), attorney's fees, and in cases involving willful infringement or trade secret theft, criminal penalties.

For student engineers, the most common IP risk areas are:

  • Using patented components or processes in a design without a license
  • Copying software libraries or code without complying with the applicable open-source license
  • Reproducing images, diagrams, or text from published sources without attribution or permission (copyright infringement)

Fair use is a legal doctrine that allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission — for purposes such as education, commentary, criticism, or research. However, fair use is a defense evaluated case-by-case, not a blanket exemption. When in doubt about whether a use is permissible, cite the source, seek permission, or find an openly licensed alternative.

IP Violation Example Potential Consequence Patent infringement Selling a device that uses a patented mechanism without a license Civil lawsuit; injunction; damages Copyright infringement Reproducing a textbook diagram in a report without permission DMCA takedown; civil lawsuit Trademark infringement Using a competitor's logo on your product Injunction; reputational damage Trade secret theft Sharing an employer's proprietary process with a competitor Criminal charges; civil lawsuit

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
This chapter covered the foundational work of starting an engineering project responsibly. Here is what you should be able to explain and apply:

Defining the Problem:

  • Engineers begin with careful problem identification — not every frustration is a viable engineering problem, but those with measurable impact and a definable scope are.
  • A needs assessment confirms the problem is real and helps you understand it at the root cause level.
  • Opportunity recognition reframes problems as specific, actionable design opportunities with named beneficiaries.
  • An engineering challenge definition captures the situation, need, measurable impact, and goal in a single focused statement.
  • Constraints (non-negotiable limits) and criteria (desired qualities) define the design space before brainstorming begins.
  • Brainstorming uses deferred judgment to generate many ideas before evaluating any of them.
  • Project management applies throughout: scope, schedule, resources, risk, and communication are all managed from day one.

Engineering Ethics:

  • Engineering codes of ethics universally place public safety and welfare as the paramount obligation.
  • Professional responsibility means taking ownership of the safety, accuracy, and consequences of your work.
  • Ethical decision making is a four-step process: recognize, gather facts, evaluate options, act and reflect.
  • Professional ethics are a minimum floor that governs conduct regardless of personal preferences.
  • Conflicts of interest must be disclosed promptly and managed to preserve professional integrity.

Intellectual Property:

  • Patents protect inventions for 20 years in exchange for public disclosure.
  • Copyrights automatically protect original creative expression from the moment of creation.
  • Trademarks protect brand identifiers that distinguish products in the marketplace.
  • Trade secrets protect confidential information through secrecy rather than registration.
  • IP infringement carries civil and sometimes criminal consequences — understanding what you may and may not use is a professional obligation.

All of these concepts apply directly to your practicum project. Your problem statement is due as the first deliverable. Make it specific, measurable, and grounded in a real need.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Before committing to a design direction, engineers must evaluate whether their solution is safe and identify what could go wrong. This chapter teaches students to apply safety standards to their designs, systematically identify risks, analyze their likelihood and impact, and develop strategies to reduce or eliminate them — a critical step before the project proposal is written.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text: Risk Management — CTE-51, CTE-62

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 5 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Safety Standards
  2. Design Safety Evaluation
  3. Risk Identification
  4. Risk Analysis
  5. Risk Mitigation Strategies

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination
  • Chapter 5: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Every engineering design carries some degree of risk. A bridge might fail under an unexpected load. A medical device might malfunction at a critical moment. A software system might leak private data. Engineers do not eliminate risk entirely — that is often impossible — but they are professionally and ethically obligated to identify risks, understand them, and reduce them to an acceptable level before any design is built or deployed.

Risk management is not a bureaucratic formality. It is one of the most direct expressions of the engineering code of ethics you studied in Chapter 5: placing the safety, health, and welfare of the public above all other considerations. This chapter gives you the tools to do that work systematically for your own practicum project.

docs.title: Safety Standards
docs.text:
A safety standard is a documented set of requirements, guidelines, or specifications that defines acceptable performance for an engineering design in a given context. Safety standards are developed by government agencies, professional engineering organizations, and industry bodies — not individual engineers — precisely because safety decisions should not depend on one person's judgment alone.

Before you can evaluate your design against a standard, you need to know which standards apply to your project. The governing standard depends on the domain your project touches:

Domain Example Standards Body Example Standard Electrical systems UL (Underwriters Laboratories), IEEE UL 62368-1 (audio/video equipment) Structural / civil ASCE (American Society of Civil Engineers) ASCE 7 (minimum design loads) Mechanical / pressure ASME (American Society of Mechanical Engineers) ASME B31.3 (process piping) Consumer products CPSC (Consumer Product Safety Commission) 16 CFR Part 1500 (hazardous substances) Environmental EPA National Ambient Air Quality Standards Medical devices FDA, ISO ISO 13485 (quality management for medical devices)

For high school practicum projects, your design will rarely need to comply with a formal regulatory standard — but understanding which standard would apply in a real deployment helps you think like a professional engineer.

docs.title: Design Safety Evaluation
docs.text:
Design safety evaluation is the process of systematically comparing a proposed engineering design against relevant safety standards and criteria to determine whether the design is acceptable before resources are committed to building it. Catching a safety problem at the design stage costs far less — in time, money, and potentially human harm — than discovering it after construction or deployment.

The core technique is a structured checklist review. For each component or function of your design, you ask:

  • Does this component meet the applicable safety standard?
  • What happens if this component fails? Does failure cascade to other parts?
  • Are there guards, interlocks, or redundancies that prevent or contain failure?
  • Are the materials, tolerances, and energy levels within accepted safe ranges?

Two of the most widely used formal safety evaluation methods in professional engineering are FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) and FTA (Fault Tree Analysis). FMEA works bottom-up: you list every component, identify every way it could fail, and trace the effects of that failure through the system. FTA works top-down: you start with a catastrophic outcome you want to prevent and work backward to find combinations of events that could cause it. Both methods are more rigorous than a simple checklist, and both appear in engineering practice in regulated industries like aerospace, automotive, and medical devices.

For your practicum project, a structured design safety checklist — rather than a full FMEA — is the appropriate tool. Your checklist should address materials safety, energy hazards, user interaction points, failure modes for critical components, and environmental impact.

docs.title: Diagram: FMEA Process Walkthrough
